subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Two-Year Limitation for Equalization Claim Under FLA s.7(3)
fact_pattern: Donna and Raymond separated in January 2021. They attempted reconciliation for several months, failing permanently in October 2021. Raymond never commenced divorce proceedings. In March 2026, Donna files an application for equalization of net family property under the Family Law Act. Raymond argues her claim is statute-barred under the FLA's specific limitation period.
call_of_question: What limitation period governs Donna's equalization claim under the Family Law Act, and when does it begin to run?

options.A: Donna's equalization claim is subject to the general two-year limitation under the Limitations Act, 2002, running from the date of separation in January 2021; her claim filed in March 2026 is statute-barred.
options.B: Under FLA s.7(3), a claim for equalization of net family property must be commenced within two years after the day the marriage is terminated by divorce or judgment of nullity, within two years after the day a separation agreement is entered into, or within six years after the day the spouses separate and there is no reasonable prospect of resumption of cohabitation; Donna's claim must be analyzed under these specific triggering events.
options.C: There is no limitation period for equalization claims in Ontario; they may be brought at any time during or after the marriage.
options.D: The limitation period for equalization is ten years from separation.

explanation: Under the Family Law Act s.7(3) provides three specific limitation periods for equalization claims: (a) two years after the day the marriage is terminated by divorce or judgment of nullity; (b) two years after the day a separation agreement is entered into; or (c) six years after the day the spouses separate in circumstances that appear to be permanent. These are specific limitation periods under the FLA that displace the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period. On the facts, if there is no divorce and no separation agreement, Donna must rely on the six-year period from the date of permanent separation (October 2021, when the reconciliation finally failed). If the permanent separation date is October 2021, the six-year period runs to October 2027, meaning Donna's March 2026 application is within time. Raymond's argument based on the January 2021 date may fail if the permanent separation date is found to be October 2021.
why_A_wrong: The general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period does not govern equalization claims. FLA s.7(3) contains specific limitation periods that displace the general Act. The FLA's six-year period from permanent separation is the applicable limitation here.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.7(3) provides three specific limitation triggers — divorce, separation agreement, or six years from permanent separation; the general Limitations Act does not apply; Donna's claim may be in time if permanent separation is October 2021.
why_C_wrong: FLA s.7(3) expressly imposes limitation periods for equalization claims. The claim is not unlimited in time; the six-year period from permanent separation applies where there is no divorce or separation agreement.
why_D_wrong: There is no ten-year limitation for equalization claims. FLA s.7(3) provides a maximum of six years from permanent separation (absent divorce or separation agreement), not ten.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Equalization Claim Limitation — FLA s.7(3)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 7(3)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Limitation Period for Matrimonial Home Possession Orders
subtopic_heading: Limitation Period for Exclusive Possession Application — FLA s.19
fact_pattern: Peter and Joan separated in 2020. Joan remained in the matrimonial home with the children. No exclusive possession order was ever sought. The home is registered in both names. Peter recently announced he plans to sell the home and wants Joan to vacate. It is now 2026. Joan consults a lawyer about whether she can now apply for exclusive possession.
call_of_question: Is Joan's potential application for exclusive possession of the matrimonial home subject to a limitation period, and what law governs?

options.A: There is no limitation period for exclusive possession applications; Joan may apply at any time regardless of how long the parties have been separated.
options.B: The general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 applies to exclusive possession applications, running from the date Joan knew or ought to have known her right to possession was threatened; a court would assess when the claim was discovered.
options.C: The limitation period for exclusive possession is six years from the date of separation under FLA s.7(3).
options.D: Exclusive possession orders are not available more than one year after separation.

explanation: Under Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19, Unlike equalization claims, which have specific limitation periods under FLA s.7(3), there is no equivalent specific limitation period in the FLA for exclusive possession applications. Courts have applied the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period to exclusive possession claims. The two-year period runs from the date the claim was discovered — that is, when Joan knew or ought to have known that: (a) she had a right to apply for exclusive possession; and (b) a proceeding was an appropriate means to enforce that right. The discovery date may be the date of separation, or it may be later — such as when Peter first threatened to sell the home or demanded Joan vacate. Joan should seek advice urgently as the limitation period may apply.
why_A_wrong: Exclusive possession applications are civil claims subject to limitation periods under the Limitations Act, 2002. They are not exempt from limitation periods. Joan's delay of 6 years raises serious limitation concerns.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic period applies to exclusive possession claims, running from discovery; Peter's recent threat to sell may extend the discovery date.
why_C_wrong: FLA s.7(3) applies specifically to equalization of net family property claims, not to exclusive possession applications. There is no equivalent six-year special period for possession claims.
why_D_wrong: There is no one-year cutoff for exclusive possession orders in the FLA. The applicable limitation is the general two-year period under the Limitations Act, 2002 from the date of discovery.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period — Exclusive Possession Applications
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4

tested_concepts: no specific FLA limitation for possession claims

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Limitation Periods for Spousal Support and Child Support Claims Compared
fact_pattern: Lucy and David separated in March 2021. Lucy did not apply for spousal support at the time. She also did not apply for child support for their three children, relying instead on informal arrangements. By March 2026, she consults a lawyer about both claims. The lawyer must advise whether both claims may be out of time.
call_of_question: What is the limitation period for a spousal support claim under the FLA compared to a child support claim under the Divorce Act or FCSG?

options.A: Both spousal support and child support claims under the FLA are subject to the same two-year limitation period with no exceptions.
options.B: Spousal support under the FLA is subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation from discovery; however, child support claims benefit from special protection — under the FCSG, no limitation period restricts the court from making an initial child support order at any time, as the child's right to support is paramount and may not be waived by the delay of a parent.
options.C: Both spousal support and child support claims have no limitation period whatsoever; they may be brought at any time.
options.D: Child support is subject to a six-year limitation period; spousal support has no limitation period.

explanation: Under Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part III, the limitation period framework for support claims in family law is asymmetric. Spousal support claims under FLA Part III are subject to the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period from discovery. A claim to obtain an initial spousal support order may be time-barred if the claimant unreasonably delayed bringing the application. Child support, by contrast, is treated differently: courts have consistently held that delay by a custodial parent in seeking child support does not disentitle the child from support. Child support belongs to the child, not the parent, and a parent's failure to apply promptly does not bar an initial child support order. Lucy's child support claim can still be made; her spousal support claim faces a more serious limitation argument, depending on when her claim was discovered.
why_A_wrong: Child support and spousal support are not treated identically for limitation purposes. Child support benefits from a more protective approach due to the child's independent right to support; spousal support is subject to the general two-year Limitations Act period.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Spousal support faces a two-year limitation from discovery; child support is not limited by parental delay — the child's right to support cannot be extinguished by a parent's delay.
why_C_wrong: Spousal support claims under the FLA are subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period. They are not unlimited in time. Child support is more protected from limitation arguments, but the positions differ.
why_D_wrong: There is no six-year limitation period for child support. Child support claims benefit from a policy against limiting the child's right to support through parental delay — this is not a specific six-year limitation, but a broader policy of not extinguishing child support through limitation defences.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period — Spousal Support vs Child Support
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part III; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4; Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175

tested_concepts: spousal support two-year limitation from discovery
child support not barred by parental delay

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Limitation Periods for Child Support Claims
subtopic_heading: Child Support Under the Divorce Act — No Limitation Period on Initial Application
fact_pattern: A child is 16. The parents divorced when the child was 5 and no child support order was ever made. The custodial parent now applies under the Divorce Act for a child support order. The other parent argues the claim is statute-barred because it has been more than 11 years since the divorce, and Ontario's general limitation period of two years has long expired.
call_of_question: Is a first-time application for child support under the Divorce Act subject to a limitation period that would bar the claim?

options.A: Yes — the general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 bars the claim; 11 years is far beyond the limitation period.
options.B: No — under the Divorce Act, there is no limitation period that bars an initial application for child support; a court may make a child support order at any time; child support is the right of the child and parental delay cannot extinguish it.
options.C: Yes — applications for child support under the Divorce Act must be brought within six years of the divorce.
options.D: No — but only if the parent is claiming on behalf of an Indigenous child; limitation periods on child support are waived for Indigenous children only.

explanation: Under Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.1, There is no limitation period under the Divorce Act that bars an initial application for child support. The Divorce Act (DA ss.15.1) confers jurisdiction on a court to make a child support order at any time during the marriage, upon divorce, or afterward. Unlike spousal support (where the two-year limitation under the Limitations Act, 2002 may apply to delay), child support is treated differently because it belongs to the child — not the parent. A parent's delay in applying does not extinguish the child's right to support. Courts may consider retroactivity separately (using the DBS v SRG framework), but the claim itself is not time-barred. The two-year Limitations Act, 2002 period does not apply to override the DA's jurisdiction to grant child support.
why_A_wrong: The Limitations Act, 2002 general two-year period does not bar initial child support applications under the Divorce Act. Child support belongs to the child and cannot be extinguished by a parent's procedural delay. No limitation period bars the initial application.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No limitation period bars an initial child support application under the Divorce Act; child support is the child's right; parental delay does not extinguish the initial entitlement.
why_C_wrong: There is no six-year period from divorce limiting child support applications under the Divorce Act. The DA's child support jurisdiction is not subject to limitation periods that would bar an initial application.
why_D_wrong: The absence of a limitation period on initial child support applications applies to all children regardless of cultural background; it is not limited to Indigenous children. The principle is that child support belongs to the child and cannot be waived or extinguished by parental delay.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Child Support Limitation — No Bar Under Divorce Act
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.1; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B

tested_concepts: no limitation period on initial child support application under DA
parental delay does not bar application

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Limitation Periods for Child Support Claims
subtopic_heading: Arrears of Child Support — Limitation Period for Enforcement of Judgment
fact_pattern: A child support order was made eight years ago requiring the payor to pay $1,200/month. The payor fell behind on payments and has accumulated $72,000 in arrears. The recipient wants to enforce the arrears through court proceedings. The payor argues that enforcement of arrears is subject to the general two-year limitation period, which has expired for older arrears.
call_of_question: What limitation period applies to the enforcement of child support arrears in Ontario?

options.A: The two-year general limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 bars enforcement of arrears that are more than two years old.
options.B: Under the Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 and the FRSAEA enforcement framework, there is no limitation period for proceedings to enforce a court order; once child support arrears accrue under a court order, they constitute a court-order debt that can be pursued at any time — neither the two-year basic period nor any other limitation period bars enforcement; the FRO may enforce arrears without a new court order.
options.C: There is no limitation period on enforcement of child support arrears under any circumstances.
options.D: Enforcement of arrears must commence within one year of each missed payment or the right to enforce that payment is lost.

explanation: Under Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 16, Child support arrears under a court order are a form of judgment debt. The Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 provides that there is no limitation period in respect of a proceeding to enforce a court order. This means that child support arrears arising under a court order can be enforced at any time — there is no two-year or ten-year bar on enforcement of a court order's arrears. The Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996 (FRSAEA) provides additional enforcement mechanisms through the FRO (Family Responsibility Office), including licence suspension and credit reporting, without any limitation bar on the arrears. The payor's argument that a two-year limitation applies is incorrect in the context of enforcing an existing court order's arrears.
why_A_wrong: The two-year general Limitations Act, 2002 period does not apply to enforcement of arrears under an existing court order. Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 excludes limitation periods for proceedings to enforce court orders.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Arrears under a court order are enforceable under the Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 framework — court order enforcement has no limitation period; FRO may pursue arrears at any time.
why_C_wrong: Option C is overbroad — the correct statement is that no limitation period applies specifically to enforcement of a court order under Limitations Act, 2002 s.16; where child support has not yet been reduced to a court order (e.g., only a private agreement), different limitation analysis may apply. Option C's 'under any circumstances' language is too absolute, whereas Option B provides the precise statutory basis (s.16) and the correct context (court-order arrears).
why_D_wrong: There is no one-year per-payment limitation rule for child support arrears. Enforcement of the full accumulated arrears is not time-barred by each missed payment becoming separately unenforceable after one year.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period — Child Support Arrears Enforcement
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 16; Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31

tested_concepts: Limitations Act 2002 s.16 no limitation on court order enforcement
child support arrears enforceable without limitation bar
two-year period does not apply to court order arrears

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Interaction of Divorce Act and FLA Limitation Periods
subtopic_heading: FLA vs DA Jurisdiction — Married Parties May Always Use DA to Vary FLA Support Orders
fact_pattern: An Ontario court made a spousal support order under the Family Law Act (not the Divorce Act) in 2018. The parties were married at the time but had not divorced. The parties divorced in 2020. In 2026, the recipient seeks to vary the 2018 FLA spousal support order. The payor argues the variation must proceed under the FLA, not the Divorce Act, because the original order was made under the FLA.
call_of_question: Must the variation of a FLA spousal support order for married parties proceed under the FLA, or may the recipient proceed under the Divorce Act?

options.A: The variation must proceed under the FLA because the original order was made under that statute; the DA is unavailable.
options.B: Once the parties are divorced, they may bring a variation application under either the Divorce Act s.17 or the Family Law Act, at their election, as both statutes are available to formerly married spouses.
options.C: After divorce, the Divorce Act supersedes the Family Law Act; a divorced spouse who wishes to vary a prior FLA support order may do so under the Divorce Act s.17, as the DA is the governing federal legislation for married and formerly married persons; the court may also convert the FLA order into a DA corollary relief order.
options.D: The variation must proceed under the FLA regardless of the subsequent divorce; the DA only applies to original applications made simultaneously with the divorce.

explanation: Under the Divorce Act is federal legislation that applies to married and formerly married persons. Once the parties are divorced, the DA provides an independent basis for support and variation applications, even if the original support order was made under the provincial FLA. A divorced spouse may bring a variation application under DA s.17 in respect of a prior FLA support order, and the court may either treat it as a DA variation or convert the FLA order into a DA corollary relief order. The DA does not require that the original order have been made under the DA; it confers an independent statutory basis for variation in respect of support for divorced persons. This means divorced spouses have access to the DA's variation regime regardless of which statute governed the original order.
why_A_wrong: After divorce, the parties may access the Divorce Act for variation of support obligations regardless of which statute governed the original order. The FLA original order does not permanently confine the parties to the FLA for future variation.
why_B_wrong: While both statutes may technically be available in some circumstances, the better answer is that the DA supersedes the FLA for formerly married persons; the DA becomes the primary statutory vehicle for variation of support for divorced persons, and the court may convert the FLA order into a DA order.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. After divorce, DA s.17 provides an independent basis for variation of prior FLA support orders; the DA supersedes the FLA for formerly married persons; the court may convert the FLA order to a DA corollary relief order.
why_D_wrong: The Divorce Act is not limited to applications made simultaneously with the divorce. DA s.17 specifically contemplates post-divorce variation applications, which may be brought at any time after the divorce is granted.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: DA Supersedes FLA for Divorced Persons — Variation of FLA Orders Under DA
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part III

exam_trigger_words: FLA order converted to DA order
tested_concepts: DA s.17 available to vary FLA orders post-divorce
DA supersedes FLA for divorced persons
court may convert FLA order to DA order

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Limitation Periods for Common-Law Partner Claims
subtopic_heading: Common-Law Spousal Support Limitation — FLA s.29 Two-Year Limitation
fact_pattern: Nina and Oscar cohabited for 4 years in a conjugal relationship and separated in March 2022. Nina did not apply for spousal support at the time of separation. In April 2024, Nina consults a lawyer and asks about a spousal support claim under the FLA. Oscar argues the claim is statute-barred.
call_of_question: What is the limitation period for a common-law spousal support claim under the Family Law Act, and has it expired for Nina?

options.A: There is no limitation period for spousal support claims under the FLA; claims may be brought at any time.
options.B: Under FLA Part III and the Limitations Act, 2002, a claim for spousal support by a common-law partner must be brought within two years of separation; Nina separated in March 2022 and her April 2024 consultation is approximately two years later — she is at or near the limitation period and must act immediately.
options.C: Common-law support claims are subject to the same limitation periods as equalization claims under FLA s.7(3).
options.D: Common-law partners have six years from separation to bring a support claim.

explanation: Under Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29, Spousal support claims by common-law partners under the Family Law Act (Part III) are subject to the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period. For FLA s.29 support claims by common-law spouses, the limitation period runs from the date of separation — when the claimant knew or ought to have known they had a claim for support. Nina and Oscar separated in March 2022. As of April 2024, Nina is approximately two years post-separation. She is at the edge of the limitation period and must act immediately to preserve her claim. If any time beyond April 2024 passes before an application is issued, the claim may be statute-barred. The FLA s.7(3) specific limitation periods apply only to equalization of net family property, not to support claims.
why_A_wrong: Common-law spousal support claims are subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period. They are not unlimited in time. Nina's delayed application creates a real limitation risk.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA Part III support claims by common-law partners are governed by the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period; Nina is at or near the limit as of April 2024 and must act immediately.
why_C_wrong: FLA s.7(3) limitation periods apply to equalization of net family property — a right available only to married spouses. Common-law partners have no equalization rights and the s.7(3) periods do not govern their support claims.
why_D_wrong: There is no six-year limitation period for common-law spousal support claims. The applicable period is the general two-year Limitations Act, 2002 period from discovery. The six-year period in FLA s.7(3) applies to equalization claims by married spouses.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Common-Law Spousal Support Limitation
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Limitation Periods for Common-Law Partner Claims
subtopic_heading: Common-Law Property Claims — Constructive Trust Under Limitations Act, 2002
fact_pattern: Gillian and Derek cohabited for 10 years and separated in January 2020. Gillian made significant contributions to Derek's home during the relationship. In February 2023, Gillian brings a claim for unjust enrichment and a constructive trust over Derek's home. Derek argues the claim is statute-barred under the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year limitation period.
call_of_question: Are common-law property claims based on unjust enrichment and constructive trust subject to the Limitations Act, 2002, and does the discovery rule apply?

options.A: Common-law property claims based on unjust enrichment are not subject to any limitation period; they may be brought at any time.
options.B: Under the Limitations Act, 2002, unjust enrichment and constructive trust claims by common-law partners are subject to the two-year basic limitation period from the date the claim was 'discovered'; the claim is discovered when the claimant knew or ought to have known she had a claim, that the claim is appropriate to pursue, and the identity of the respondent; discovery typically occurs at separation when the unjust enrichment becomes apparent.
options.C: Common-law property claims are governed by the ten-year ultimate limitation period only; the two-year basic period does not apply.
options.D: Constructive trust claims have a six-year limitation period matching the FLA equalization limitation.

explanation: Under Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4, s. 15, Unjust enrichment and constructive trust claims by common-law partners are civil claims governed by the Limitations Act, 2002. The basic limitation period is two years from the date the claim was discovered. Discovery occurs when the claimant knew or ought to have known: (a) that the act or omission occurred; (b) that the claimant suffered a loss from it; (c) that the claim is appropriate to pursue; and (d) the identity of the respondent. For common-law property claims, discovery typically occurs at separation when the unjust enrichment crystallizes and the parties' contributions to the property become clear. Gillian and Derek separated in January 2020; her February 2023 claim is approximately three years post-separation — she must argue that the discovery of her claim was delayed beyond January 2020 for any reason to avoid limitation.
why_A_wrong: Common-law property claims are civil claims governed by the Limitations Act, 2002. They are subject to limitation periods. The absence of a specific FLA limitation period for these claims means the general Limitations Act, 2002 framework applies.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Unjust enrichment claims are subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic period from discovery; discovery typically occurs at separation; Gillian's 2023 claim three years after separation creates a real limitation risk.
why_C_wrong: The ten-year ultimate limitation period applies regardless of discovery (s.15 of the Limitations Act, 2002), but the two-year basic limitation from discovery also applies and is the operative bar in most cases. Both periods apply; the two-year basic period is the primary concern.
why_D_wrong: There is no six-year limitation period for constructive trust claims. The FLA s.7(3) six-year period applies only to equalization of net family property for married spouses. Constructive trust claims by common-law partners are governed by the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic period.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Common-Law Property Claims — Unjust Enrichment Limitation
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4, s. 15

tested_concepts: Limitations Act 2002 applies to unjust enrichment claims
FLA s.7(3) inapplicable to common-law property

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: FLA s.50 — Spousal Support Arrears as Judgment Debt
fact_pattern: A spousal support order made under the Family Law Act requires Paul to pay $2,000/month. Paul paid sporadically and has accumulated arrears of $60,000 over five years. Mary now wants to enforce the accumulated arrears. Paul argues that arrears more than two years old are statute-barred by the Limitations Act, 2002.
call_of_question: Are accumulated spousal support arrears under an FLA order subject to the two-year general limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002?

options.A: Yes — each missed monthly payment creates a separate two-year limitation period; arrears more than two years old are unenforceable.
options.B: No — under FLA s.50 and the Limitations Act, 2002 s.16, once a support payment falls due under a court order and becomes part of the arrears, it constitutes a judgment debt; enforcement of court order debts is not subject to the two-year basic limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 s.16; Mary may enforce all accumulated arrears.
options.C: Support arrears are subject to a special four-year limitation period under the FLA.
options.D: Support arrears may only be enforced through the Family Responsibility Office and not through the courts after two years.

explanation: Under Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 50, Spousal support arrears accumulate as a debt arising under a court order. The Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 provides that no limitation period applies in respect of a proceeding to enforce a court order. Once monthly support payments fall due and are not paid, they accrue as arrears — a form of court-order debt. Because s.16 removes the limitation bar for enforcing court orders, Mary may enforce the full $60,000 in accumulated arrears without any two-year time bar. FLA s.50 allows accumulated arrears to be enforced as a judgment. The Family Responsibility Office (FRO) may also pursue these arrears without limitation. Paul's argument based on the two-year Limitations Act, 2002 period is incorrect for court order debts.
why_A_wrong: Each missed monthly payment does not create a separate two-year limitation bar. The Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 excludes limitation periods for proceedings to enforce court orders. All accumulated arrears under a court order are enforceable regardless of when they arose.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.50 and Limitations Act, 2002 s.16 establish that court order debts including support arrears are not subject to the two-year limitation bar; Mary may enforce the full accumulated arrears.
why_C_wrong: There is no special four-year limitation period for FLA support arrears. The applicable rule is Limitations Act, 2002 s.16, which removes the limitation bar entirely for enforcement of court orders.
why_D_wrong: Support arrears may be enforced both through the Family Responsibility Office and through court proceedings. Neither route is time-limited by the two-year Limitations Act, 2002 period. Both enforcement mechanisms remain available.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: FLA Spousal Support Arrears — No Limitation Bar
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 50; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 16

tested_concepts: FLA s.50 arrears as judgment debt
Limitations Act 2002 s.16 court order enforcement no limitation
two-year period does not apply to court order debts
